title: Designing a swale or rain garden
subtitle: Capturing and infiltrating roof and surface runoff in the garden
excerpt: A swale is a level-bottomed depression that slows surface water and encourages infiltration. A rain garden is a planted depression designed to receive roof runoff and allow it to soak in. Both reduce runoff entering the sewer and store water for dry periods.
sourceNotes: CIRIA SuDS Manual C753. RHS guidance on rain gardens and sustainable drainage. Waterwise UK surface water management guidance for domestic gardens.

content.text: and a rain garden solve the same problem from different directions. A swale moves water slowly across a landscape and allows it to infiltrate along its length. A rain garden receives water in one location and allows it to infiltrate through a planted, permeable area. Both are alternatives to piping surface water straight to a drain or watercourse, and both create habitats that support more plant diversity than a conventional lawn or border.

Both features depend on the soil draining adequately. Test your soil permeability: dig a hole 300 mm deep, fill with water, and measure how quickly it drains. Water draining faster than 25 mm per hour is suitable. Water that barely drains over several hours indicates clay soil that will not support a standard rain garden; a raised bed design with imported free-draining soil is needed instead. Do not locate a rain garden within 3 m of the house foundations or 1 m of a boundary.

Size the rain garden to absorb runoff from the contributing surface. For roof runoff from a standard down pipe serving 25 to 50 m² of roof, a rain garden of 4 to 8 m² (planted area) with a saucer depth of 100 to 200 mm is sufficient. Calculate: (roof area in m²) x (design storm depth in mm) = litres to absorb. The UK design storm for garden-scale features is typically 15 mm (the 1-in-5-year event); a 50 m² roof in a 15 mm storm generates 750 litres.

Plants must put up with both wet periods and dry ones between storms. A rain garden has zones. The lowest, wettest zone suits moisture-loving plants like Iris sibirica, Juncus effusus, Persicaria, and Lobelia cardinalis. The higher edges suit plants that like free drainage but can take the odd flooding, such as Geranium, Achillea, Salvia, and ornamental grasses. Do not plant lawn grass in the lowest zone. It dies in long wet spells.
Connecting a downpipe to a rain garden

redirects flow from the downpipe when a water butt overflows or when the butt is bypassed. A short below-ground pipe (90 mm drain pipe) leads from the diverter to a gravel-filled inlet channel in the rain garden. The inlet channel distributes flow across the garden without eroding the soil.
to a rain garden. Roof runoff is relatively clean; greywater contains detergents, skin cells, and pathogens that damage the soil biology the feature depends on.

attrs.body: Always design an overflow route for storms larger than the design event. An overflow channel at the edge of the rain garden should direct excess water to a suitable location (lawn, permeable path, soakaway) rather than toward the building or neighbour's boundary. Without an overflow, the first extreme rainfall event can flood the area around the feature.
attrs.title: Overflow provision
